quiz_questions.question_text: What is homeostasis?

options.text: The process of maintaining a stable internal environment despite changes in external conditions
The ability of organisms to grow larger over time
The process of adapting to a new environment permanently
The breakdown of nutrients for energy production

quiz_questions.explanation: Homeostasis is the maintenance of a stable internal environment (temperature, pH, blood glucose, water balance, etc.) despite fluctuations in external conditions. It relies on feedback loops — primarily negative feedback — that detect deviations from a set point and trigger corrective responses. All living organisms must maintain homeostasis to survive; failure leads to disease or death.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a negative feedback loop?

options.text: A regulatory mechanism where the response opposes the initial stimulus to bring a variable back to its set point
A mechanism that amplifies a stimulus until a process is complete
A response that only occurs during illness or injury
A process that permanently changes the body's set point

quiz_questions.explanation: Negative feedback is the most common homeostatic mechanism. When a variable (e.g., body temperature, blood glucose) deviates from its set point, receptors detect the change, a control center (e.g., hypothalamus) processes the signal, and effectors produce a response that opposes the deviation. For example: body temperature rises → hypothalamus triggers sweating and vasodilation → temperature drops back toward 37°C. The response reverses the stimulus.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the normal human body temperature set point?

options.text: Approximately 37°C (98.6°F), maintained by the hypothalamus through thermoregulation
Exactly 40°C (104°F) at all times
35°C (95°F), which is the threshold for hypothermia
Variable between 30-42°C depending on the person's age

quiz_questions.explanation: The hypothalamus acts as the body's thermostat, maintaining core temperature near ~37°C (98.6°F). When temperature rises above the set point, the hypothalamus triggers cooling responses (sweating, vasodilation). When it drops below the set point, heating responses activate (shivering, vasoconstriction, piloerection). This is a classic negative feedback loop with receptors (thermoreceptors), a control center (hypothalamus), and effectors (sweat glands, blood vessels, muscles).

quiz_questions.question_text: What role does insulin play in blood glucose homeostasis?

options.text: Insulin is released by beta cells when blood glucose is high, signaling cells to absorb glucose and the liver to store it as glycogen
Insulin raises blood glucose by breaking down glycogen in the liver
Insulin is released when blood glucose is low to stimulate hunger
Insulin destroys excess glucose molecules in the bloodstream

quiz_questions.explanation: After a meal, rising blood glucose is detected by beta cells in the pancreatic islets of Langerhans. They release insulin, which signals body cells (muscle, liver, fat) to take up glucose from the blood. The liver converts excess glucose to glycogen (glycogenesis). As blood glucose falls back to the set point (70-100 mg/dL fasting), insulin secretion decreases — a negative feedback loop. Glucagon from alpha cells has the opposite effect when glucose is too low.

quiz_questions.question_text: How does the body respond when core temperature drops below the set point?

options.text: Shivering generates heat, vasoconstriction reduces heat loss from the skin, and piloerection traps insulating air
Sweating increases and blood vessels dilate to release excess heat
The kidneys produce more urine to warm the body through metabolic activity
The heart rate slows dramatically to conserve energy

quiz_questions.explanation: When thermoreceptors detect a drop in core temperature below ~37°C, the hypothalamus activates heat-conserving and heat-generating responses: (1) Shivering — rapid involuntary muscle contractions that produce heat; (2) Vasoconstriction — narrowing of blood vessels near the skin surface reduces heat radiation; (3) Piloerection — contraction of arrector pili muscles raises body hairs, trapping an insulating layer of air. These mechanisms work together as a negative feedback loop to restore temperature to the set point.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the role of glucagon in blood glucose homeostasis?

options.text: Glucagon is released by alpha cells when blood glucose is low, signaling the liver to break down glycogen into glucose (glycogenolysis)
Glucagon lowers blood glucose by signaling cells to absorb glucose
Glucagon is released when blood glucose is high to store excess sugar
Glucagon is produced by the hypothalamus to regulate body temperature

quiz_questions.explanation: Glucagon is the antagonistic partner to insulin. When blood glucose drops below the set point (between meals or during fasting), alpha cells in the pancreatic islets release glucagon. It signals the liver to break down stored glycogen into glucose (glycogenolysis) and to produce new glucose from amino acids and other substrates (gluconeogenesis). This raises blood glucose back to normal. Insulin and glucagon work as an antagonistic hormone pair.

quiz_questions.question_text: What organ acts as the body's thermostat, controlling temperature homeostasis?

options.text: The hypothalamus — it receives input from thermoreceptors and coordinates heating or cooling responses
The cerebellum — it controls all involuntary body functions
The thyroid gland — it directly raises or lowers body temperature
The skin — it independently decides when to sweat or shiver

quiz_questions.explanation: The hypothalamus is the primary control center for thermoregulation. It receives signals from peripheral thermoreceptors (in the skin) and central thermoreceptors (in the hypothalamus itself, blood). When temperature deviates from the set point (~37°C), the hypothalamus activates appropriate effectors: cooling mechanisms (sweating, vasodilation) when hot, or heating mechanisms (shivering, vasoconstriction) when cold.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is positive feedback, and how does it differ from negative feedback?

options.text: Positive feedback amplifies a stimulus rather than opposing it, driving a process to completion (e.g., childbirth contractions)
Positive feedback maintains variables at a stable set point just like negative feedback
Positive feedback is the most common homeostatic mechanism in the body
Positive feedback always reduces a variable back to normal

quiz_questions.explanation: While negative feedback opposes change to maintain a set point (most homeostatic loops), positive feedback amplifies change, pushing a process to completion. Examples are rare: during childbirth, the baby's head pushing on the cervix triggers oxytocin release → stronger contractions → more pressure → more oxytocin, escalating until delivery. Other examples include blood clotting (platelet aggregation) and lactation (suckling → prolactin → more milk). Positive feedback always needs a terminating event.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the role of ADH (antidiuretic hormone) in water homeostasis?

options.text: ADH is released by the posterior pituitary when the body is dehydrated, making kidney collecting ducts more permeable to water to reduce urine output
ADH causes the kidneys to excrete more water when the body has excess fluid
ADH is produced by the kidneys to regulate blood pressure
ADH increases sweating to remove excess water from the body

quiz_questions.explanation: ADH (antidiuretic hormone, also called vasopressin) is produced by the hypothalamus and released from the posterior pituitary. When blood osmolarity rises (dehydration), osmoreceptors trigger ADH release. ADH inserts aquaporin-2 water channels into kidney collecting duct cells, allowing more water to be reabsorbed back into the blood. This produces concentrated, low-volume urine. When the body has excess water, ADH decreases, and dilute, high-volume urine is produced.

quiz_questions.question_text: What are the three components of a feedback loop?

options.text: Receptor (sensor that detects change), control center (processes information and determines response), and effector (carries out the response)
Stimulus, hormone, and target organ only
Brain, spinal cord, and peripheral nerves only
Input, processing, and storage

quiz_questions.explanation: Every feedback loop has three essential components: (1) Receptor/sensor — detects changes in a variable (e.g., thermoreceptors detect temperature changes, osmoreceptors detect blood concentration changes); (2) Control center — receives and processes receptor input and determines the appropriate response (e.g., hypothalamus for temperature, pancreas for glucose); (3) Effector — carries out the corrective response (e.g., sweat glands cool the body, muscle cells absorb glucose). The effector's action feeds back to the receptor, completing the loop.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a set point in homeostasis?

options.text: The target or normal value for a physiological variable that the body works to maintain (e.g., body temperature ~37°C, blood pH 7.35-7.45)
The maximum value a physiological variable can reach before death
A fixed value that never changes under any circumstances
The lowest level of a variable during sleep

quiz_questions.explanation: A set point is the ideal or target value around which a variable is maintained. For example: body temperature ~37°C, blood glucose 70-100 mg/dL fasting, blood pH 7.35-7.45, blood osmolarity ~300 mOsm/L. The body uses negative feedback loops to detect deviations from the set point and activate corrective mechanisms. Some set points can be adjusted — for example, fever temporarily raises the thermoregulatory set point through pyrogen action on the hypothalamus.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is thermoregulation?

options.text: The homeostatic process of maintaining a stable body temperature through mechanisms like sweating, shivering, vasodilation, and vasoconstriction
The process of generating heat only during exercise
The regulation of water temperature in aquatic environments
The ability to change body temperature to match the environment

quiz_questions.explanation: Thermoregulation is the homeostatic maintenance of body temperature near the set point (~37°C in humans). Humans are endotherms — we generate internal heat through metabolism. When overheated: vasodilation (blood vessels dilate to radiate heat) and sweating (evaporative cooling). When cold: vasoconstriction (blood vessels constrict to conserve heat), shivering (muscle contractions generate heat), and piloerection (goosebumps — less effective in humans than in furry animals). The hypothalamus orchestrates all these responses.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is osmoregulation?

options.text: The homeostatic regulation of water and solute concentrations in body fluids, primarily through kidney function and hormones like ADH and aldosterone
The regulation of oxygen levels in the blood
The process of digesting and absorbing nutrients
The immune system's response to osmotic stress

quiz_questions.explanation: Osmoregulation is the active regulation of water and solute balance to maintain proper osmolarity (~300 mOsm/L in human blood). The kidneys are the primary osmoregulatory organs, adjusting urine concentration and volume. Key hormones include: ADH (increases water reabsorption when dehydrated), aldosterone (promotes Na⁺ reabsorption and K⁺ excretion), and ANP (promotes Na⁺ and water excretion when blood volume is too high). Failure of osmoregulation leads to dehydration, edema, or dangerous electrolyte imbalances.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the bicarbonate buffer system?

options.text: The primary blood buffer system where carbonic acid (H₂CO₃) and bicarbonate (HCO₃⁻) neutralize excess acids or bases to maintain blood pH at 7.35-7.45
A system that produces bicarbonate to help digest food in the stomach
A kidney filtration mechanism that removes all acids from the blood
A liver enzyme system that breaks down lactic acid directly

quiz_questions.explanation: The bicarbonate buffer system (CO₂ + H₂O ↔ H₂CO₃ ↔ H⁺ + HCO₃⁻) is the most important extracellular buffer. When excess H⁺ enters the blood (acidosis), bicarbonate ions bind them, forming carbonic acid, which dissociates into CO₂ (exhaled by lungs) and water. When the blood becomes too basic (alkalosis), the equilibrium shifts to produce more H⁺. The lungs regulate CO₂ levels (fast response) and kidneys regulate bicarbonate levels (slow, powerful response), creating a coordinated system.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is vasodilation?

options.text: The widening of blood vessels near the skin surface, allowing more blood flow to the skin to release heat through radiation
The narrowing of blood vessels to conserve body heat
The contraction of muscles to generate heat
The dilation of airways to increase oxygen intake

quiz_questions.explanation: Vasodilation is the widening of blood vessel diameter, particularly arterioles near the skin surface. During overheating, the hypothalamus triggers smooth muscle in vessel walls to relax, increasing blood flow to the skin. This brings warm blood close to the surface, where heat is lost through radiation, convection, and conduction. The opposite process — vasoconstriction (vessel narrowing) — occurs in cold conditions to reduce heat loss by keeping warm blood away from the skin surface.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a pyrogen?

options.text: A substance (produced by immune cells or pathogens) that raises the body's temperature set point in the hypothalamus, causing fever
A chemical that lowers body temperature during exercise
A hormone that destroys pathogens directly
A type of white blood cell that fights infection

quiz_questions.explanation: Pyrogens are fever-inducing substances. Endogenous pyrogens (interleukin-1, interleukin-6, TNF, prostaglandins) are released by immune cells during infection. Exogenous pyrogens come from pathogens themselves (e.g., bacterial lipopolysaccharide). They act on the hypothalamus to raise the thermoregulatory set point. The body then uses its normal heating mechanisms (shivering, vasoconstriction) to reach the new, higher set point. Fever is thus a controlled homeostatic adjustment, not a failure of thermoregulation.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is aldosterone?

options.text: A steroid hormone from the adrenal cortex that promotes sodium reabsorption and potassium excretion in the kidneys, helping regulate blood pressure and fluid balance
A hormone from the posterior pituitary that controls water reabsorption
A digestive enzyme produced by the pancreas
A neurotransmitter released by the hypothalamus

quiz_questions.explanation: Aldosterone is a mineralocorticoid produced by the zona glomerulosa of the adrenal cortex. It acts on the distal convoluted tubule and collecting duct of the nephron, promoting Na⁺ reabsorption (water follows osmotically, raising blood volume and pressure) and K⁺ secretion. Aldosterone is part of the RAAS (renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system): low blood pressure → kidneys release renin → angiotensinogen → angiotensin I → angiotensin II → stimulates aldosterone release. Addison's disease (low aldosterone) causes hyponatremia and hyperkalemia.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is metabolic acidosis?

options.text: A condition where blood pH drops below 7.35 due to excess acid or loss of bicarbonate, triggering compensatory hyperventilation to expel CO₂
A condition where blood pH rises above 7.45 due to excessive vomiting
A normal response to eating acidic foods like citrus
A type of respiratory failure caused by lung disease

quiz_questions.explanation: Metabolic acidosis occurs when blood pH falls below 7.35 due to accumulation of metabolic acids (lactic acid, ketoacids in diabetic ketoacidosis) or loss of bicarbonate (severe diarrhea). The respiratory system compensates by increasing ventilation (Kussmaul breathing) to expel CO₂, shifting the buffer equation (CO₂ + H₂O ↔ H₂CO₃ ↔ H⁺ + HCO₃⁻) leftward, reducing H⁺. The kidneys provide slower but more powerful compensation by excreting H⁺ and regenerating bicarbonate.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is erythropoietin (EPO)?

options.text: A hormone produced by the kidneys in response to low blood oxygen that stimulates red blood cell production in the bone marrow
A white blood cell that fights infection in the lungs
A protein produced by the liver that carries oxygen in the blood
An enzyme that breaks down old red blood cells in the spleen

quiz_questions.explanation: Erythropoietin (EPO) is a glycoprotein hormone produced primarily by the kidneys (and to a lesser extent the liver) when they detect hypoxia (low oxygen). EPO travels to the bone marrow and stimulates erythropoiesis — the production and maturation of red blood cells. This is a homeostatic response: more RBCs → more hemoglobin → greater oxygen-carrying capacity. This is why altitude adaptation increases EPO, and why EPO doping is banned in sports. Chronic kidney disease causes anemia because the kidneys can't produce adequate EPO.

quiz_questions.question_text: What are aquaporins?

options.text: Water channel proteins embedded in cell membranes that allow rapid osmotic water movement, regulated by ADH in the kidney collecting ducts
Sodium pumps that actively transport salt across cell membranes
Enzymes that break down water molecules during cellular respiration
Antibodies that protect aquatic organisms from infection

quiz_questions.explanation: Aquaporins are integral membrane proteins that form water-selective channels, allowing rapid osmotic water transport across cell membranes. In the kidney, aquaporin-2 (AQP2) is regulated by ADH: when ADH binds to receptors on collecting duct cells, vesicles containing AQP2 fuse with the apical membrane, dramatically increasing water permeability and reabsorption. When ADH decreases, AQP2 is internalized, and the collecting duct becomes impermeable to water, producing dilute urine.

quiz_questions.question_text: A marathon runner's core temperature climbs to 39.5°C during a race in hot weather. Describe the complete negative feedback loop that the body uses to cool down, identifying the receptor, control center, and effectors.

options.text: Peripheral and central thermoreceptors detect elevated temperature → the hypothalamus (control center) processes the signal → effectors activate: vasodilation increases blood flow to skin for heat radiation, and sweat glands produce sweat for evaporative cooling → temperature falls toward 37°C set point
Muscles detect heat → cerebellum signals → vasoconstriction and shivering → temperature drops
Skin detects heat → spinal cord signals → goosebumps form → temperature stabilizes
The pituitary detects heat → releases ADH → kidneys excrete warm urine → temperature drops

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a textbook thermoregulatory negative feedback loop with all three components: (1) Receptors — thermoreceptors in the skin (peripheral) and hypothalamus (central) detect the temperature increase; (2) Control center — the anterior hypothalamus processes the input and determines the response; (3) Effectors — sweat glands produce sweat (evaporative cooling removes ~2.4 MJ/L of sweat evaporated) and arterioles near the skin vasodilate (increased blood flow radiates heat). As temperature returns toward 37°C, receptor stimulation decreases, and the response diminishes — the hallmark of negative feedback.

quiz_questions.question_text: A patient with Type 1 diabetes cannot produce insulin. After eating a carbohydrate-rich meal, their blood glucose rises to 350 mg/dL. Why does this represent a homeostatic failure, and what metabolic crisis can result if untreated?

options.text: Without insulin, cells cannot absorb glucose despite high blood levels — the body breaks down fat instead, producing ketone bodies (acids), leading to diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) with dangerously low blood pH
Without insulin, glucagon is overproduced, which lowers blood glucose to dangerous levels
The high glucose directly damages the pancreas, stopping all hormone production
Cells absorb glucose normally without insulin, so no crisis occurs

quiz_questions.explanation: Type 1 diabetes is autoimmune destruction of pancreatic beta cells, eliminating insulin production. The negative feedback loop is broken: glucose rises but cannot be cleared. Paradoxically, despite blood glucose being very high, cells are 'starving' because they can't absorb glucose without insulin signaling. The body responds by breaking down fat via lipolysis, producing acetyl-CoA in excess, which is converted to ketone bodies (acetoacetate, beta-hydroxybutyrate, acetone). These are acids that overwhelm the bicarbonate buffer system, causing DKA — a life-threatening emergency with pH <7.3, dehydration, and altered consciousness.

quiz_questions.question_text: A patient in the ICU has blood pH of 7.25 (metabolic acidosis). The respiratory and renal systems both compensate, but at different speeds. Compare the two compensation mechanisms and explain why the respiratory response is faster.

options.text: Respiratory compensation occurs within minutes — increased ventilation expels CO₂, shifting the buffer equation leftward to consume H⁺. Renal compensation takes hours to days — kidneys excrete H⁺ directly and regenerate bicarbonate, providing more powerful but slower correction
Renal compensation is faster because the kidneys directly remove acid; the lungs can only adjust CO₂
Both systems respond at the same speed — within seconds of detecting pH change
The liver compensates fastest by converting all acids to bile for excretion

quiz_questions.explanation: pH homeostasis uses a tiered response system: (1) Chemical buffers (bicarbonate system) act in seconds; (2) Respiratory compensation (Kussmaul breathing — deep, rapid breathing) acts within minutes — by expelling CO₂, it shifts CO₂ + H₂O ↔ H₂CO₃ ↔ H⁺ + HCO₃⁻ leftward, reducing H⁺. This is fast because breathing rate can change almost instantly; (3) Renal compensation takes hours to days but is more powerful — the kidneys can excrete H⁺ into urine, reabsorb/regenerate bicarbonate (HCO₃⁻), and produce new bicarbonate from glutamine metabolism. The combined system normally keeps pH within the very narrow 7.35-7.45 range.

quiz_questions.question_text: A doctor treats a patient with diabetes insipidus, a condition where the kidneys do not respond to ADH. The patient produces up to 20 liters of dilute urine per day. Explain why this occurs using the ADH feedback loop.

options.text: Without functional ADH signaling, aquaporin-2 channels are not inserted into collecting duct membranes — water cannot be reabsorbed from the filtrate, so nearly all filtered water passes into the urine as extremely dilute output
The kidneys overproduce ADH, which forces excessive water excretion
The patient drinks too much water, and the kidneys excrete the excess normally
Aldosterone excess causes the kidneys to excrete sodium, dragging water with it

quiz_questions.explanation: Diabetes insipidus breaks the ADH-water reabsorption feedback loop. Normally: dehydration → osmoreceptors detect high osmolarity → posterior pituitary releases ADH → ADH binds V2 receptors on collecting duct cells → aquaporin-2 (AQP2) channels inserted into apical membrane → water reabsorbed → concentrated urine. In nephrogenic diabetes insipidus, the kidneys are resistant to ADH (receptor or AQP2 defects). In central diabetes insipidus, the pituitary doesn't produce ADH. Either way, collecting ducts remain impermeable to water. The kidneys produce ~180 L of filtrate daily; normally ~99% is reabsorbed. Without ADH, much more passes into urine (up to 20 L/day), causing severe dehydration and extreme thirst (polydipsia).

quiz_questions.question_text: An athlete collapses during a hot-weather marathon with core temperature at 41°C and has stopped sweating. How does heat stroke differ from fever in terms of homeostatic mechanism, and why is cessation of sweating particularly dangerous?

options.text: Fever is a controlled set-point adjustment (pyrogens raise the hypothalamic set point), while heat stroke is homeostatic failure — cooling mechanisms are overwhelmed. Cessation of sweating removes the body's most effective cooling mechanism (evaporative cooling), causing uncontrolled temperature rise
Both fever and heat stroke involve the hypothalamus intentionally raising temperature; they differ only in severity
Heat stroke occurs because the skin produces too much sweat, not too little
Fever is a homeostatic failure while heat stroke is a controlled response

quiz_questions.explanation: This question distinguishes controlled homeostatic adjustment from true homeostatic failure. In FEVER: pyrogens raise the hypothalamic set point → body uses normal heating mechanisms (shivering, vasoconstriction) to reach the new, higher target → homeostasis is maintained at a different set point. In HEAT STROKE: external/metabolic heat overwhelms compensatory mechanisms → sweating failure occurs due to dehydration (no fluid for sweat) or autonomic dysfunction → without evaporative cooling (each liter of sweat evaporated removes ~2.4 MJ of heat), temperature spirals upward → this is genuine homeostatic breakdown, a medical emergency requiring immediate external cooling to prevent organ damage and death.

quiz_questions.question_text: A patient with chronic kidney disease has blood test results showing elevated urea, elevated potassium (6.2 mEq/L), low bicarbonate, and low erythropoietin. Explain how kidney failure disrupts multiple homeostatic systems simultaneously.

options.text: The kidneys regulate waste excretion (urea builds up), electrolyte balance (K⁺ cannot be excreted → hyperkalemia), acid-base balance (cannot excrete H⁺ or regenerate bicarbonate → acidosis), and erythropoiesis (cannot produce EPO → anemia) — failure disrupts all simultaneously
Kidney failure only affects water balance; the other abnormalities are caused by liver failure
The adrenal glands compensate for all kidney functions when the kidneys fail
Only urea is affected; electrolytes and hormones are regulated by the intestines

quiz_questions.explanation: The kidneys are arguably the most important homeostatic organs, maintaining multiple variables simultaneously: (1) Waste excretion — urea and creatinine accumulate (uremia causes nausea, confusion); (2) Electrolyte balance — K⁺ normally excreted in urine builds up (hyperkalemia >6 mEq/L can cause fatal cardiac arrhythmias); (3) Acid-base — kidneys can't excrete H⁺ or regenerate HCO₃⁻ (metabolic acidosis); (4) Erythropoietin — reduced EPO → decreased RBC production (renal anemia); (5) Fluid balance — water retention → edema, hypertension; (6) Calcium/phosphate via vitamin D activation. This is why dialysis must replace multiple kidney functions.

quiz_questions.question_text: A mountaineer at 5,500 meters altitude experiences immediate responses (hyperventilation, tachycardia) followed by long-term adaptations over weeks. What specific homeostatic adaptation involves the kidneys, and why does this improve oxygen delivery to tissues?

options.text: The kidneys detect chronic hypoxia and increase erythropoietin (EPO) production → EPO stimulates bone marrow to produce more red blood cells → more hemoglobin → greater oxygen-carrying capacity of blood
The kidneys excrete excess oxygen in the urine to prevent oxygen toxicity
The kidneys shrink at altitude to reduce oxygen consumption
The kidneys increase blood plasma volume to dilute the blood

quiz_questions.explanation: At altitude, partial pressure of O₂ is lower, reducing hemoglobin saturation. Immediate responses (increased heart rate and breathing) provide acute compensation but are energetically costly. The key long-term adaptation involves the kidneys: specialized peritubular cells detect hypoxia → upregulate hypoxia-inducible factor (HIF) → increase EPO gene transcription → more EPO in blood → bone marrow increases erythropoiesis. Over 2-3 weeks, RBC count rises significantly, increasing blood hemoglobin concentration and oxygen-carrying capacity. This is why endurance athletes train at altitude, and why EPO doping is prohibited in sports.

quiz_questions.question_text: A patient develops Conn's syndrome (primary hyperaldosteronism), where the adrenal glands produce excessive aldosterone. Using the RAAS feedback loop, predict the patient's blood pressure, sodium level, and potassium level.

options.text: High blood pressure (hypertension), high sodium (hypernatremia), and low potassium (hypokalemia) — because excess aldosterone causes excessive Na⁺ reabsorption (water follows → volume expansion) and excessive K⁺ excretion
Low blood pressure, low sodium, and high potassium — the opposite of aldosterone's effects
Normal blood pressure because renin suppression compensates completely
High calcium and low phosphate because aldosterone regulates bone minerals

quiz_questions.explanation: Aldosterone promotes Na⁺ reabsorption and K⁺ secretion in the distal nephron. In Conn's syndrome, autonomous excess aldosterone production causes: (1) Excessive Na⁺ reabsorption → water follows osmotically → blood volume expands → hypertension (the most common presentation); (2) Excessive K⁺ secretion → hypokalemia (can cause muscle weakness, cardiac arrhythmias); (3) Increased H⁺ secretion → metabolic alkalosis. The elevated blood pressure would normally suppress renin release (negative feedback), and renin IS low in Conn's syndrome — but this can't stop the autonomous aldosterone secretion from the adrenal tumor. This illustrates what happens when a component of a feedback loop becomes unregulated.

quiz_questions.question_text: A person with chronic liver disease develops hepatic encephalopathy (confusion and altered consciousness). Blood tests show elevated ammonia levels. Explain the failed homeostatic mechanism, tracing the pathway from amino acid metabolism to brain dysfunction.

options.text: The liver normally detoxifies ammonia (from amino acid deamination) into urea via the urea cycle → in liver failure, ammonia accumulates in blood → crosses the blood-brain barrier → astrocytes convert it to glutamine, causing osmotic swelling → cerebral edema and neurotoxicity → encephalopathy
The liver normally stores ammonia as glycogen; when the liver fails, glycogen breaks down into toxic ammonia
Ammonia comes from the brain itself when the liver stops sending glucose
The kidneys produce ammonia in liver failure because they take over urea production

quiz_questions.explanation: This traces a homeostatic failure from molecular biochemistry to organ dysfunction: (1) Amino acid metabolism produces ammonia (NH₃) as a byproduct — ~30-50 grams daily from protein digestion and muscle metabolism; (2) Normally, the liver's urea cycle converts toxic NH₃ → non-toxic urea (CO(NH₂)₂), which is excreted by the kidneys; (3) In liver failure, urea cycle capacity is overwhelmed → blood ammonia rises (hyperammonemia); (4) Ammonia crosses the blood-brain barrier → astrocytes attempt to detoxify it by converting glutamate + NH₃ → glutamine (via glutamine synthetase); (5) Glutamine accumulation causes osmotic swelling of astrocytes → cerebral edema; (6) Additionally, the glutamate/glutamine imbalance disrupts neurotransmission. Treatment includes lactulose (traps NH₃ in gut as NH₄⁺) and rifaximin (reduces ammonia-producing gut bacteria).

quiz_questions.question_text: During childbirth, oxytocin creates a positive feedback loop. A student asks: 'If positive feedback keeps amplifying, why doesn't the loop continue forever?' Explain what terminates the positive feedback loop and why positive feedback is essential here but dangerous if uncontrolled.

options.text: Delivery of the baby and placenta removes the stimulus (pressure on the cervix), terminating the loop — positive feedback is essential because labor requires escalating force, but all positive feedback loops require a terminating event or they become pathological
The hypothalamus eventually stops producing oxytocin after a set time period regardless of stimulation
Negative feedback from progesterone automatically overrides the positive feedback
Positive feedback loops always resolve on their own after 24 hours

quiz_questions.explanation: Positive feedback during childbirth: baby's head pushes on cervix → stretch receptors signal hypothalamus → posterior pituitary releases oxytocin → uterine muscle contractions intensify → baby pushed harder against cervix → more oxytocin → stronger contractions. This escalating cycle is necessary because delivering a baby requires progressively stronger forces. The TERMINATING EVENT is delivery — once the baby and placenta are expelled, cervical pressure disappears, the stimulus is removed, and oxytocin release decreases. This illustrates a critical principle: all positive feedback loops require an external terminating event. Without one (as in pathological blood clotting or cytokine storms), positive feedback becomes a dangerous, runaway process. This is why positive feedback is rare in physiology — most homeostasis relies on negative feedback, which is inherently self-limiting.
